prompt:
Our archive sweep is useless on the share it was built for and I'm out of ideas. The setup: a nightly collector walks the NAS and writes a listing, one file per line, byte count, tab, path (a stat failure becomes -1 for the count). sweep.c reads that listing and prints the archive candidates -- everything at or over the threshold, biggest first, sizes in whole MiB.

On the pilot share (nothing bigger than a couple of gig) it was flawless. On the real NAS:

- At our standard 4 GiB threshold the report comes back COMPLETELY EMPTY. The listing plainly has five- and six-gig disk images in it. I checked the collector's byte counts against the share by hand; the input is right.
- At a 2 GiB threshold some of the big files do show up, but with nonsense sizes -- a 6.5 G video mastering file printed as "2560 MiB" -- and two files that are absolutely over 2 GiB aren't in the report at all.
- On a different share the sizes looked fine but the ordering came out scrambled, big things sorted after smaller ones.

Same listing in, same wrong report out, every run, so at least it's consistent. The tests in test_sweep.c carry a fixture listing with the hand-checked byte counts and pin the exact reports we should be getting at both thresholds. `make test` must pass. Makefile, mintest.h and test_sweep.c stay as they are -- fix the sweep itself, and fix it for real; I don't want a version that only works below some magic file size.
